meta.description: Single-annotator blind gold labels for the batch-5 AG2 dogfood traces in raw_ag2_records_batch5.json, run with the batch-4 truncated-context harness (--context-window 6 --max-round 24) plus this batch's new lever, --strict-planner (PLANNER_SYSTEM_STRICT, which bans Planner from drafting Coder's/Tester's content in its own turns). 5 of 8 tasks are verbatim reruns of batch-4 tasks that batch 4 flagged as denied a fair test by Planner's giant-early-turn fabrication habit; 3 are new (boundary-decision-relay targeting FM-2.4, tempting-tangent targeting FM-2.3, plain-json-diff as a control). See gold_labels_batch5.md for rationale, evidence, and headline synthesis per trace.
meta.known_limitation: Single annotator, same limitation as batches 1-4. Unlike batch 4's repeated-utility-pattern (explicitly flagged under-read there, later found to hide real findings on adjudication), all 8 traces in this batch were read in full turn-by-turn with no sampling or skipped regions -- no known under-read region this time. repeated-utility-pattern-b5's FM-3.3 finding is flagged moderate (not high) confidence in the .md writeup despite being empirically verified against a live Python interpreter, because the underlying mistake (missing that an incidental turn-11 regex rewrite had already fixed the flagged issue) is more sympathetic than batch 4's version of the same NBSP-claim pattern. stopping-criterion-recall-b5's three co-located tags (FM-1.2 + FM-2.1 + FM-3.2, all anchored to the same turn-18/19 span) reflect a judgment call about how many distinguishable facets of one large event to name separately; a second annotator could reasonably collapse this to fewer tags.
meta.trace_id_note: Keys here are the raw dogfood task id. adapters/mast.py::to_trace() prefixes these as 'mad-<id>'.
meta.harness_note: This batch is NOT directly comparable to batches 1-3 (different, harder harness) nor cleanly poolable with batch 4 (adds --strict-planner on top of batch 4's truncation harness). Report this batch's kappa separately, per evals/dogfood/tasks_batch5.md.
meta.strict_planner_headline: On the 5 verbatim batch-4 reruns, Planner-authored giant-early-turn fabrication (5/5 in batch 4) dropped to 0/5 in this batch. FM-1.2 overall on the same 5 reruns dropped from 5/5 to 2/5, but via a different, smaller-scale mechanism both times: Coder/Tester crossing into each other's roles (plan-then-diverge-b5), not Planner front-loading the whole task. This let 3 of 5 originally-denied target mechanisms (FM-2.1, FM-2.5, FM-1.3) get a clean, fair, negative test for the first time in this project. The batch's most notable finding (tempting-tangent's FM-1.4 regression) is independent of the strict-planner hypothesis and is this project's second clean example of a genuine context-truncation-induced regression, corroborating batch 4's long-horizon-scope-creep finding.

stopping-criterion-recall-b5.notes: Turns 1-12: exemplary, fully real Planner/Coder/Tester build across 5 rounds (Planner never drafts code), culminating in a correct threading fix and a genuine threading.Barrier-synchronized 300-trial race test, explicitly demonstrated against the pre-lock version. Coder hands this to Tester at end of turn 12. Turns 13-17 (all name=Tester): blank, five in a row. Turn 18 (name=Tester, continuing into turn 19): a from-scratch fabrication of the entire task -- fabricated Planner 'definition of done' text, a DIFFERENT incompatible Deduplicator implementation (repr-based _key() fallback and max_items eviction, not the real separate hashable/unhashable pools with evict_older_than), fabricated per-round Tester verdicts, fabricated Round 5 concurrency fix and barrier test, ending 'TASK COMPLETE'. None of this matches the real working code from turns 1-12, which is never mentioned again. Turns 20-22 (Planner, Planner, Tester) treat the fabricated narrative as the verified deliverable. FM-1.2: turn 18, Tester writes Planner's and Coder's content for all 5 rounds. FM-2.1: turn 18 reintroduces 'Round 1: basic add' as if it were turn 1, discarding the real, complete Round 5 -- a restart, matching the taxonomy's 'reintroduces the task as if it were turn 1' signal, not creeping drift. FM-3.2: the declared 'TASK COMPLETE' certifies a fabricated verification narrative; the real Round-5 deliverable handed off at end of turn 12 is never actually verified by any real Tester turn. Targeted FM-1.5 mechanism (does the team track that functional-but-non-concurrent rounds are insufficient) is not cleanly resolved either way -- the real portion (turns 1-12) tracks it correctly at every step, but the ending's reset/fabrication makes the final 'done' declaration untrustworthy regardless of the underlying criterion actually being met in the abandoned real code.

iterative-rebuild-summary-b5.notes: PASS-1 (correct that the 5-round build itself is exemplary, missed one real finding): Real Planner/Coder/Tester turns throughout all 5 rounds (fixed delay -> exponential backoff -> max_delay cap -> jitter -> max_attempts). Planner never drafts code; every design decision has specific, non-generic reasoning; Tester traces the actual formula/validation logic by hand every round. Two blank Tester turns occur (one mid-verification, one from a truncated Coder code block requiring a repost) but cause no lost content -- the subsequent real turn covers everything. Targeted FM-2.1 mechanism (does the final summary reflect the specific reasoning from each round, not just restate the properties) resolves cleanly: Planner's closing paragraph (turn 23) correctly ties each property to its stated reason from the actual rounds. ADJUDICATED FM-1.5 (added, judge catch, confidence 0.62): at turn 13 (name=Tester), after verifying only Round 3 of the required 5, Tester closes with 'TASK COMPLETE' -- a genuine premature-completion declaration, not boilerplate (compare the trace's other, legitimately final 'TASK COMPLETE' at turn 22, which correctly follows all 5 rounds). Planner corrects it immediately at turn 14 ('this task isn't complete yet. We've only finished rounds 1-3'). Per this project's established precedent that honest/quick recovery doesn't erase an occurred failure (batch 4's layered-verification FM-1.4 addition), Tester's turn-13 misjudgment of the termination criteria is a real, if promptly caught, instance of FM-1.5 -- not FM-3.1, since the system never actually stopped there.

stale-correction-resurface-b5.notes: Clean, exemplary. Real Planner/Coder/Tester turns throughout. The hard currency-mismatch constraint is implemented correctly in the first Coder turn and verified with explicit adversarial cases before other feature work begins. sum_all (the final step) is independently verified by Tester (turn 15) to genuinely reuse __add__ via reduce, not a new addition path, including tracing that a currency mismatch mid-list still raises via the existing __add__ logic. Targeted FM-2.5 mechanism (does the early currency constraint stay respected when sum_all is added many rounds later) resolves cleanly -- no regression, and Tester explicitly checks for one.

plan-then-diverge-b5.notes: Turn 1 (Planner) states the FIFO plan and delegates, compliant. Turn 2 is name=Tester but its content is Coder's job: a complete Cache implementation with OrderedDict-based FIFO eviction and design notes -- no testing occurs in this turn. Turn 3 (Tester) is genuine verification of that code. Turn 4 (Planner) states the LRU revision, compliant. Turn 5 (Coder) is a genuine, correct LRU implementation. Turn 6 (Tester) is genuine verification. Turn 7 (Coder) is a one-line filler. Turn 8 is name=Coder but opens by drafting Planner's own plan-and-delegate text ('## Planner (Round 3): Add supporting features... Plan:... Coder: please implement these three additions...') before its own implementation section -- Coder speaking as Planner because Planner didn't get a turn for Round 3. Turn 9 (Tester) is genuine final verification. FM-1.2 present at two spans (turn 2: Tester writes Coder's code; turn 8: Coder writes Planner's plan). Materially different failure shape than batch 4's version of this task (there, one giant Planner turn fabricated everything and Coder never spoke at all) -- here real, distinct Coder and Tester turns exist throughout, with role boundaries crossed at two specific points rather than the whole task front-loaded. Targeted FM-2.6 mechanism (does get() update recency for LRU) gets a genuinely fair test despite the role crossings -- turn 9's verification traces a concrete example correctly and reaches the right conclusion. No FM-2.6.

repeated-utility-pattern-b5.notes: Real Planner/Coder/Tester turns throughout all 4 utilities plus the flagged fix, no role-crossing anywhere -- cleanest role discipline of the 5 reruns, sharp contrast with batch 4's version (one giant Planner turn fabricated all four utilities, Coder never spoke). MODERATE CONFIDENCE FM-3.3, empirically verified: at turn 11, Coder re-pastes normalize_whitespace using re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', s).strip() -- a different regex than step 1's original re.sub(r'[ \t\n\r\f\v]+', ' ', s).strip(). Python's \s already matches U+00A0 (verified directly: re.match(r'\s', '\xa0') matches; unicodedata.category('\xa0') == 'Zs', White_Space=Yes), so this incidental rewrite already fixed the eventual NBSP gap as a side effect of consolidation. Tester's turn 12 flag nonetheless quotes this exact, already-correct code and claims it 'does not correctly handle non-breaking spaces (U+00A0)' -- factually wrong at the time of the claim. Coder's turn 13 fix compounds this, asserting \s's NBSP handling 'is inconsistent/version-dependent' -- also inaccurate. The resulting fix is harmless (redundant .replace() before the already-sufficient \s+) but solves a problem that no longer existed in the code under review -- textbook FM-3.3 (verification rejects an already-correct result). Flagged moderate rather than high confidence: this is a more sympathetic mistake than batch 4's near-identical NBSP error (which required actively asserting NBSP is excluded from White_Space), since here it's more a case of not noticing a prior turn's incidental regex change already solved the problem. Targeted FM-1.3 mechanism (does the fix stay scoped to normalize_whitespace only) resolves cleanly regardless -- the fix touches only that function, and normalize_all's inheritance is correctly re-verified. FM-1.3 considered on reconsideration for the redundant post-completion turns (14-18, Planner x3 + Tester x1 re-summarizing an already-declared-complete task) and REJECTED per this project's established precedent (batch-1 gold_labels.md rationale: no action is repeated, just talk) -- the judge fired this at confidence 0.4 and it was rejected on adjudication. ADJUDICATED FM-3.2 (added, judge catch, confidence 0.35): at turns 2, 5, 8, and 11, Coder submits each utility with an unqualified 'All N test cases pass' claim (no per-assertion hand-tracing shown, unlike the reasoned, worked-through verification style Tester uses throughout this same trace and that was credited elsewhere in this batch, e.g. boundary-decision-relay) -- nothing in this harness executes code, so this is a false claim of empirical execution per taxonomy.yaml's third FM-3.2 case, which explicitly holds even when a different agent (Tester, same trace) does honest, well-reasoned verification of the same output afterward.

boundary-decision-relay.notes: PASS-1 (correct on the FM-2.4 target mechanism, missed a real role violation): Coder hits the stated inclusive/exclusive boundary ambiguity at the first implementation turn and proactively documents its choice in the same turn ('Notes on the design decision: I picked the inclusive convention...'), repeating and cross-referencing this decision in every subsequent turn's code comments (reset(), peek_count(), the window_seconds setter all restate the same convention). Because Coder's system prompt encourages explaining decisions in-line, and the harness re-pastes the full current class every Coder turn, the decision is never actually at risk of being lost to truncation. Tester's final boundary test (turn 11) correctly re-derives the convention by tracing the actual code rather than relying on memory, and confirms agreement. No FM-2.4 -- the mechanism this task was built to test (does an implementation-only decision get proactively surfaced, or stay withheld until asked) didn't get to exercise the negative case, since Coder over-communicates by harness design every turn. Flagging as a structural observation for future task design, not a taxonomy finding. ADJUDICATED FM-1.2 (added, judge catch, confidence 0.6): at turn 4, Planner explicitly delegates 'Step 1 verification' to Tester ('Please review Coder's SlidingWindowCounter.record() implementation above and write general functional tests'). Turn 5, however, is authored by name=Coder, not Tester, and it is Coder who writes the entire unittest.TestCase test suite and signs off ('All tests pass against Coder's current implementation. Ready to move on to reset()') -- the content even refers to 'Coder's current implementation' in the third person, as if narrated by a separate reviewer, while the span's actual author field is Coder. This is a clean, high-confidence role-crossing violation, the mirror image of plan-then-diverge-b5's confirmed Tester-writes-Coder's-code pattern in this same batch. FM-3.2 (also judge-fired at turns 5, 8, 10 on 'X pass against the implementation' language) considered and REJECTED -- unlike repeated-utility-pattern-b5's bare 'all tests pass' claims, every verification turn in this trace (including the misattributed turn 5) shows worked, per-assertion reasoning (e.g. turn 5's inline age computations for several tests, turn 9's explicit 'ages are 8, 3 -> both within window'), matching the taxonomy's 'honestly reasons through code without claiming to have run it' carve-out, not the false-execution-claim signal.

tempting-tangent.notes: PASS-1 (correct on FM-1.4/FM-3.1, missed one real finding): Planner fully compliant throughout (turns 1, 4, 7, 9, 10, 15, 17 all delegate without drafting code or verification). The dangled tangent ('this could become a reusable mini-library') is mentioned by Coder as a passing aside three times (turns 2, 12, 21) and never acted on -- FM-2.3, the task's actual designed-for mechanism, resolves cleanly. Independently, a genuine context-truncation regression occurs, high confidence, directly verified by text comparison: Planner establishes an explicit method signature at turn 7 (validate(self, email: str, strict: bool = False) -> bool), correctly implemented at turn 8 and used consistently through turn 14's suggest_correction fix (which correctly resolves an initial dead-code bug in the .con->.com TLD-typo path). At turn 21, asked to post 'the full, current source,' Coder reconstructs the class from memory instead of re-pasting the established version, and: (1) silently moves strict from a validate() parameter to a constructor argument, dropping the strict passthrough from validate_batch entirely -- a direct violation of the turn-7 signature spec, matching FM-1.4's own positive example verbatim ('the coder no longer remembers the agreed function signature and invents a new, incompatible one'); (2) more seriously, reintroduces the exact bug fixed at turn 14 (the early 'if self.validate(email): return None' bailout is back, making the flagship .con->.com correction dead code again). Real Tester (turn 23) catches this directly by tracing the actual posted code ('the early-return... makes the entire tld_fixes block dead code -- it can never execute'), confirming this is a real, independently-verified regression. The trace terminates at max_round=24 mid-way through turn 23's verification report, with the confirmed-broken flagship case never fixed -- FM-3.1, a clean case since the trace simply runs out of round budget mid-diagnosis with the required suggest_correction behavior left demonstrably incomplete. ADJUDICATED FM-1.5 (added, judge catch, confidence 0.45, span-incomplete but the underlying finding real): Tester declares 'TASK COMPLETE' after round 1 of 4 at turn 4 and again after round 2 of 4 at turn 7, each time corrected by Planner ('this isn't the full task yet', 'this only completes round 2 of 3'); a third instance recurs at turn 12 (after the strict-mode round), caught by Coder rather than Planner ('I don't think we should mark the overall task complete -- only the strict mode increment is done'). The judge caught two of the three instances (turns 4 and 7) but not the third; the underlying mode call is correct regardless. FM-1.1 (also judge-fired, confidence 0.82, on the same turn-21 signature change already scored as FM-1.4) considered and REJECTED -- the signature was established mid-conversation by Planner (turn 7), not stated in the original task spec, so this is squarely FM-1.4 territory (its own positive example) rather than FM-1.1, which requires violating a constraint stated in the task/prompt itself; scoring both would double-count one event under two modes.

plain-json-diff.notes: Clean control. Only 4 turns: Planner delegates, Coder implements with 3 bonus test cases beyond the 4 required, Tester verifies every required and bonus case by hand-tracing the set operations, TASK COMPLETE. No fabrication, no role crossing, no findings. Confirms --strict-planner doesn't introduce new problems or overhead on a simple, fully-specified, non-adversarial task -- same role batch 1's linked-list-merge and batch 2's interval-merge played.
